game_description: Race your checkers home and bear them off before your opponent, 2 players.
filter_1_label: Ahead in pip count (closer to home)?

strategies.strategy_detailed: The strongest opening rolls: 3-1 (make the 5-point), 4-2 (make the 4-point), 6-1 (make the bar-point). All three make a home or near-home point immediately, building your prime. 5-3 splits to 3-point and bar-point. Avoid leaving blots on exposed high points.
strategy_stealth: Avoid blots on high exposed points

strategies.strategy_detailed: If your pip count is lower after the opening, play a running game — race for home, avoid contact. If behind in pips, play a blocking game — make points, build a prime, hit opponent blots to slow them. Pip count after the first exchange determines your strategy.
strategy_stealth: Decide after first exchange pip count

strategies.condition: Building an anchor in opponent home
strategies.strategy_detailed: An anchor (two checkers on the same point) in the opponent's home board is a powerful defensive asset. The 20-point (opponent's 5-point) is the golden anchor — from there you can re-enter quickly after being hit and threaten to hit opponent blots during their bear-off.

strategies.condition: Opportunity to hit an opponent blot
strategies.strategy_detailed: Hit blots when doing so doesn't leave your own checker exposed. A hit sends opponent to the bar and costs them an entire turn to re-enter. Always hit a blot in your home board — they must re-enter through your points. Hitting on open boards with no priming intent is riskier.
strategy_stealth: Hit when you won't be exposed
Home board hits cost opponent full turn

strategies.strategy_detailed: A 6-prime (6 consecutive points) is unpassable — opponent checkers behind it are trapped. Build your prime from the 6-point backwards. Even a 4-prime significantly reduces opponent mobility. Maintain the prime while advancing your back checkers past it.
strategy_stealth: Advance back checkers past the prime

strategies.strategy_detailed: In a clear running game, minimize wastage — avoid stacking too many checkers on low points. Spread checkers across your home board (2 per point ideally) for smooth bearing off. Each excess checker on the 6-point when you need it elsewhere is wasted pips.

Offer the double when your winning probability is above 70%. Accept a double when your winning probability is above 25% — below that it's a pass. The cube multiplies stakes: doubling too early gives opponent free leverage; doubling too late gives away equity.

A blitz attacks relentlessly while opponent is on the bar. Close your home board points one by one — the more points closed, the harder for opponent to re-enter. A closed 6-point home board with opponent on bar is nearly an automatic win. Abandon a blitz only if you can't maintain the attack.
strategy_stealth: Close home points while they're on bar

strategies.condition: Bearing off with all checkers home
strategies.strategy_detailed: Bear off efficiently: use high rolls to remove checkers from high points, lower rolls to fill gaps. If a point is empty, move a checker down from the highest occupied point — never waste a roll. Avoid leaving single checkers on high points late; they're the last to bear off.
strategy_stealth: High roll = bear off high point
Low roll = fill gaps or move down
Avoid lone checkers on high points

strategies.strategy_detailed: A gammon (opponent bears all off before you bear any) doubles the loss. If you haven't borne off any checkers and opponent is nearly done, focus entirely on bearing at least one piece off. Prioritize bearing over positioning — even one checker off prevents the gammon.
strategy_stealth: Bear over positioning if gammon risk
Gammon = double the loss — avoid it
